course_description: Theory, application and skills development in leading and managing people. Differentiating between management and leadership, organisational and team leadership, change, diversity and strategic leadership, leadership cases in Australia and global context.
timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The overall aim of this course is to prepare you personally and academically to appreciate leadership in the 21st century. More specifically, the course aims to acquaint you with relevant leadership theories and provide you with an opportunity to integrate leadership theory and practice. Besides, it aims to assist you in reflecting on the purpose and effectiveness of your business leadership and management skills and to improve your potential to lead and make a difference in the workplace.

learning_outcomes.description: Critically analyse the challenges of leading and managing people in the workplace
Critically analyse the application of various theoretical frameworks of leadership
Develop and activate a plan to build upon your strengths and weaknesses in leading and managing people
Clearly articulate the fundamental skills of leadership
Practise effective leadership & business management skills in a variety of situations

assessment_details.title: Current Issues in Leadership: Theory & Practice
assessment_details.task_description:
Individual Assignment

This is an individual assignment and it is due on or before Friday, the 22rd of May, 2015 at 4pm. The assignment (i.e. report) should be about 2000 words in length and it is worth 40% of the total course mark. The primary aim of the assignment is to enable students to see the intricate connection between leadership theory and practice. Specifically, the assignment is designed to assist students to explore the link between leadership skills, workplace crisis, diversity (multi-generations/aging workforce) and teamwork at multiple levels (i.e. individual, team and organization). Additionally, it should enable students to appreciate the current challenges about leading and managing crisis situations as well as diverse employees (including multiple generations) for productivity and wellbeing in the real world of work.

Background

In addition to the basic issues in leading and managing people, the focus of MGTS 2603 in 2015 is on current issues in leadership such as:

Leading and managing diversity such as the aging workers (e.g. their cognitive capability and productivity) especially at the individual, team and organisational level.

Leading and managing multi-generational workforce for productivity.

Leading and managing multicultural teams in public, private and global organisations and environments.

Leadership skills, leadership styles, crisis, organisational failures and performance at the individual, team and organisational level.

Leadership and or management of crisis and negative events for innovation at multiple levels (e.g. individual, team and organistional level).

The list is not exhaustive

Key Task

For the purpose of this assignment, you will choose one topic from any of the current issues in leadership listed above. Alternatively, if you would like to study some other current issues in leadership apart from the ones listed above, you need to first discuss this with your tutor and or course coordinator. If you cannot find a leader to interview, then you will need to talk to your tutor and the course co-ordinator for assistance.

Next, you will interview your preferred leader on the topic of your choice that is related to aging workforce, multi-generational teams, team & organisational diversity, crisis, failures and negative events. Preferably, you should interview a leader (organisation/team) in the organization that you are currently working with or have worked with in the recent past especially one that possibly has experienced the leading and management of aging workforce (i.e. older workers), multi-generational teams, diversity(gender, tenure), crisis and negative events or has assisted team members in overcoming the challenges of working in diverse and multigenerational environments. For example, if you choose “multi-generational workforce”, you may want to think of the generations represented in the contemporary workplace and their interactions. Also, you want to think about how their interactions may impact their well-being, productivity/performance at various organisational levels. You may also interview your leader about his/her role in managing multi-generational workforce. Specifically, you may want to know how the leader ensures that the talents, passion and energy of the young workers are effectively complemented with the experience, maturity and stablility of the older workers. Additionally, you may explore what leadership style(s) and skills that the leader needs to manage age differences in the workplace.

At the team level, you may want to interview leaders about the issues at work that may trigger crisis at the individual, team and organisational level. You may also ask the leader on how negative events or crisis have been turned around for better product development and innovation. How does the leader assist members to work through crisis and negative events. What leadership skills and syles are important in managing crisis and organisational failures? For those leaders and members who have worked through difficult emotional experience during crisis and negative circumstances, you may ask them (leader and or followers) how they overcame the trauma/difficulty. For example, you may want to ask about the processes set in place within the team to assist team members in handling these difficult emotions during crisis.

Altogether, your interview may further explore leaders' (CEOs, Premiers, Managers, Team Leaders) and team members' roles in managing and leading diversity and or crisis in industries such as Banking, Tourism/Travels, Fashion, Media Houses, Insurance companies, Governance, and Government Agencies or Parastatals. The leader you interview may have earned your admiration, trust and respect because he/she has demonstrated a formidable leadership prowess in managing the changllenges associated with diversity, crisis, and negative events in organisations. In addition, you may want to understudy (or shadow) a leader for one day and diarise how the leader manages the team or organisation for the day. The course co-ordiantor (Dr Ayoko) is working with the BEL career and recruitment unit to liase students who may want to "shadow" (under study) a leader for a day to appropriate leaders. Also, if you need a letter of introduction to the leader you want to interview, Dr Ayoko will be prepared to give you one.

The aim of the assignment is to discover the practical side of the theories/models that you have learnt in MGTS 2603. For example, you might want to ask your leader about his/her personal experience in managing negative events and crisis, diversity and multiple generations within the team, or organisation. In particular, you may want to ask the leader whether or not he/she followed any systematic process in demonstrating sensitivity to diversity (age, gender, tenure, sexual orientation and different generations at work ). Additionally, it may be worthwhile to ask your leader about his/her organisations' preparedness for crisis and how he /she has managed crisis and failures in the past at the indvidual, team and organisational level. What guidelines (if any) were followed in managing crisis or diversity-related challenges? In this respect, you may ask for what worked for him/her and what he/she would do differently in future. In sum, how did he/she manage a recent crisis, negative event or differences at work?

Report Writing Process:

Check List:

Project Topic : Decide on the topic and leader you want to interview. Then, you MUST proceed to get an approval from your tutor latest by Teaching Week 3 (i.e. within the first two tutorials). The tutors will be working together to keep a roll of those leaders who are being interviewed to ensure that no two individuals are studying exactly the same topic in the same way and interviewing the same leader. Please, refer to your learning activity guide on the course profile, textbook and journal articles for more ideas under each aspect of leadership listed above. Also, various readings are provided on the course blackboard to kick-start your assignment. Assistance will be provided in tutorials on how to draft your interview questions, prepare and conduct your interviews.

Project Backdrop : To provide a backdrop for your report, you need to interview a leader of your choice as specified above. Apart from the interview, you may also want to "shadow" your chosen leader for a day. Your purpose of understudying a leader for a day is for you to observe the leader and gain some perspectives about how a leader manages team or organisation especially in the assignment topic areas listed above. You will need to diarise your observations and may use the information from the diary as part of your backdrop for your report. If you have never worked or you are an international student, you will need to negotiate with your tutor about the leader that you can interview. Assistance will be provided if you need to be linked with a leader in a given organization within and outside Australia. You may also be able to interview a leader from your own country but you need to explore this first with Dr Ayoko, the course co-ordinator.

Theories : Decide on the underlying framework (theory/theories/models) that can best explain the aspect of leadership that you are writing about e.g. if you are writing about managing multi-generation workforce, you may need to explore theories that may assist you in understanding the interactions in multi-generational teams. You may also need to know about SWOT analysis, creating a vision, mission statement. More importantly, you need to have an understanding of how leaders’ styles, traits, skills and strategic understandings may be used to shape team processes in the context of diverse teams or how leaders skills and styles my inform effective organisational crisis management. Additionally, you need to have an understanding of how subjective and interpersonal as well as political influences may impair or promote effective diversity or crisis management.

You will also need to be familiar with leadership frameworks and or theories that help to explain challenging interactions in diverse or mulitgenerational teams/oragnisations such as Social Identity Theory- SIT- Tajfel, 1979;Tajfel & Turner,1986), transformational leadership (Bass & Avolio, 1995; Walumbwa, & Hartnell, 2011) and life span theory (Baltes, Lindenberger, & Staudinger, 2006). While you are expected to choose from the leadership theories and those presented above, you are not limited to these theories. The understanding of these theoretical issues is important for evaluating leaders’ behaviours in practice. In fact, the understanding of these theoretical issues may assist you in determining the interview questions and observation indicies for the report.

Literature Review : Background information about the organisation where your leader is coming from is compulsory. This means that you need to read about his/her organisation on such issues as their industry, industry life cycle, competitors, vision and goals, challenge, annual turnover and current industry environment in which they operate. In addition, check your organizational/team’s archives or newsletters for more information about your leaders. Then, you will need to review the literature on the topic that you have chosen. Your reviews should cover empirical findings that are relevant to your topic. It is expected that these research findings would broaden your understanding of your topic. The librarian, tutors and course coordinator will assist you in locating the relevant databases for your literature review. Please talk to them.

Assumed Report Writing Skills. MGTS2603 assumes that students are familiar with report writing. If you are in doubt, please refer to the Assignment Writing Guide and visit the course website for detailed guidance on how to structure your report.

Write your Report:

Report Outline:

Develop an outline for your report. You may want to show your report’s outline to your tutor so you can both discuss your progress in the report writing process. In your outline, ensure that you follow the format of a research report. Also, the outline should be clear about the current issue in leadership that you are writing about e.g. the focus, the theory and literature that are pertinent to your discussion.

Report Proper:

Report should articulate the following:

A clear identification of the leader that will be used as a backdrop for the report.

A clear and concise articulation of the connection between theory & practice is especially important in the area of current issues in leadership that you have chosen.

Given your chosen topic, you need a critical analysis of the leader in question. Make sure you draw clear illustrations from the interview transcripts of your selected leader. Specifically, students must bring into the body of the report, transcripts (quotes and comments) from the interviews and or observation conducted with the chosen leader. All interview transcripts should be attached to the report as an appendix and also as evidence of your interviews with the leader.

Based on your research (interviews, understudy and readings) and concepts learnt in class, the report should conclude with 3-5 recommendations for the leader that you have interviewed on how he/she can overcome his/her challenges or improve on what he/she does currently. You must include a theoretical perspective and also a relevant and current practical application of the current issue in leadership that you have chosen in the literature review section of your report.

Overall, the research report should make use of relevant theory and research evidence to critically examine the leader of your choice and how he/she portrays the aspect of leadership that you are writing about. A minimum of 10 references should be used, 8 of which should be recent academic journal articles preferably from 2005-2015. A list of relevant journals is provided on the course Blackboard.

This is a formal report and should adhere to the formats of a standard report writing and especially, you must adhere to the APA (6th Edition) for your references. This report should be double spaced and written in well-developed paragraphs (see the Assignment Writing Handbook on the course blackboard).

Research Report Writing: Suggested Steps

Step 1: Task Analysis

Take time to analyse your task. Read the background information above thoroughly and get a grasp of what is expected of you in this assignment. From time to time, the course coordinator and tutors will talk about the assignment in the lectures and in the tutorials to assist you with any questions that you might have. To get a good grasp of what the assignment is asking you to do; answer the following questions: What is the purpose of the report? Who are you writing the report for? What is the word limit? What is the topic of the report and the expected format?

Step 2: Developing a First Draft

Develop a main paragraph that clarifies the overall purpose of your report.

Review literature around your chosen topic and summarize it.

Make sure you review articles from high quality journals e.g. Journal of Organizational Behavior, Strategic Management Journal, The Leadership Quarterly, Organizational Studies, Academy of Management Journal, Academy of Management Review, Small Group Research, Journal of contingencies and crisis management, International journal of crisis management, International Journal of Conflict Management, Journal of Negotiation and Conflict Management Research, Journal of Human Resource Management, Human Resource Management International, Asia Pacifc Journal of Human Resources, Organizational Dynamics, Group Processes and Intergroup Relations, Group Dynamics: Theory, Research and Practice (see the course website for a list of more journals that you can consult). Ensure you keep a record of the correct bibliographic details of the articles you have reviewed. Write down anything else you already know under each of the relevant subheadings for report writing. NOTE: YOU MUST REVIEW THE RELEVANT LITERATURE BEFORE YOU INTERVIEW AND OR OBSERVE YOUR LEADER.

Step 3: Collect information for your Report: The Interviews.

Conduct the interviews with the chosen leader. Additionally, you may want to understudy your leader for one day and diarise your observations. Make sure that your interviews are focused on your chosen topic. One of the best ways to achieve this is to make sure that you review relevant literature before you conduct your interview or "shadow" your leader . For example, if I am interested in the role of the leader in managing the dynamics of mult-generational workforce, I will be asking questions such as: What is the nature of the interactions and processes in multi-generational teams/organisation?, What were the characteristics of the challenges experienced by multi-generational teams members?; What is the role of the leader in effectively managing the interactions and processes in multi-generational environments?, What role has your organisational or industry environment (context) played in the way your team members manage their interactions and processes related to muti-generational related issues in their organisations? Can you please tell me how your team and organisational members respond to the challenges presented by working in multi-generational teams?, On a scale of 1-10, how would you describe the success of your role in managing multi-generational team and their wellbeing?; Answers to these questions should provide an insight into the leader and team members’ management of interpersonal and team processes in multi-generational envronments. Don’t forget to record your interview with the leader (using audio tape recorder) and or diarise your one-day observation of the leader as you will need to refer to the interview transcripts and observation notes in the body of your report.

Remember to give yourself plenty of time to review the literature, conduct the interview and work through the themes or details that emerge from the interview. All of the above activities require a considerable amount of time and dedication.

Step 4: Write the body of your report:

Once you have conducted your interview and or observation, examine the transcripts and or notes to identify sentences that you can use to support your arguments in the report.

Use your literature review as a guide. For example, if the leadership literature says there is a link between leaders' behavioural styles, traits and skills (e.g. strategic capability) and the management of diversity, crisis or organisational failures, then, your interview questions should tap into ways in which your preferred leader’s behavioural styles, traits and skills (e.g. strategic capability) assist him/her in leading diversity or crisis. Your interview questions and the issues for which you observe the leader should also directly connect how your chosen leader’s behaviours are translated into employees' productivity and satisfaction or even organisational/team performance. Information from the interviews and observation notes should reveal whether or not your leader had effectively managed the crisis and diversity issues (e.g.aging workforce) and their impact on team/organisational performance.

Write up your report using a standard format of report writing e.g.

Title of report

Table of contents

Abstract (or synopsis)-refer to the Assignment Writing Guide and course website for full details.

Introduction

Literature review

Methodology

Results

Discussion

Conclusion

Recommendations

Supplementary material

References, interview scripts (Appendices)

Step 5: Write your report in full

Make sure there is a flow.

Ensure that you have proof-read your report a few times before submission.

Please note: More assistance will be provided on the course website in due course.

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myServices website ( https://student.my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.other_learning_resources:
eLearning@UQ

eLearning is used in addition to face-to-face lectures and other traditional teaching methods, and it provides additional content and communication channels delivered via the World Wide Web.

A Blackboard site is maintained for this course at http://blackboard.elearning.uq.edu.au/ . Students will be able to log in to Blackboard using their UQ username/password, i.e. the same one you use to access my.UQ, SI-Net, and student email. If your course does not appear in your list of enrolled courses on Blackboard, you should go to http://blackboard.elearning.uq.edu.au/webapps/portal/frameset.jsp?tab_id=_2_1 and see if the course is listed there. If it is not, contact the ITS Helpdesk on 3365 6000 or helpdesk@elearn.uq.edu.au .

A list of additional readings relevant to this course will be provided on the course Blackboard (BB). Also, key journal articles related to this course will be provided on the course BB. The lists provide references that are intended to complement your set textbook and recommended readings/resources to broaden your classroom learning experience. Although the lists (on the course BB) provide a representative set of high quality contemporary references from the field of leadership, they are by no means exhaustive. Students will need to do further searches to pursue particular topics in greater detail.

All courses are supported by the UQ Learning Management System, Blackboard Learn, which provides an interactive environment for the students and teachers. This is accessible via PC or Mac or Mobile (Apps) environment.

To log on to Blackboard, go to https://learn.uq.edu.au.

As Blackboard is a web‐based application, you will need a recently‐released browser for your computer.

For a current list of supported browsers see https://help.blackboard.com/en-us/Learn/9.1_SP_12_and_SP_13/Student/040_Browser_Support_for_SP_13 .

The latest browsers are available online from the UQconnect website at http://uqconnect.net/helpdesk/downloads or on the free CDs available from Ask I.T. and UQconnect desks. Contact Ask I.T. on (07) 3365 8811 http://www.library.uq.edu.au/ask‐it or UQconnect on (07) 3365 6000.

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Leading & Managing in the 21st Century (Lecture) Readings/Ref: Lussier & Achua (Ch 1 )

Leadership Traits & Ethical Behaviour (Lecture): Personality traits and leadership, traits of effective leaders. Also, ethical leadership Readings/Ref: Further Reading (Further reading:Handbook Ch 23); Lussier & Achua (Ch 2 & 10 )
Leadership Behaviours and Motivation (Lecture): Leadership behaviours and styles, leadership and major motivation theories Readings/Ref: Lussier & Achua (Ch 3)
Leadership and Influence Tactics (Lecture): Power and influencing tactics, organisational politics Readings/Ref: Further Reading (Handbook Ch 7); Lussier & Achua (Ch 4)
Contingency Theories of Leadership (Lecture): Contingency and leadership theories and models Readings/Ref: Lussier & Achua (Ch 5)

Mid-Semester Break (Independent Study): This is a good time to revise topics learnt so far for the mid-semester exam. You can also begin to prepare for your individual assignment (the report). You can do this by (a) chosing your topic and the leader you prefer to interview,(b) gathering and summarising your literature review on the topic of your choice,(c) preparing your interview questions after doing your literature review, and (d)getting ready to discuss an outline of your assignment in the next tutorial.

Leader-Follower Relations (Lecture): Leader-follower relations, dyadic linkage theory, exchange theory and followership. Readings/Ref: Further Reading (Further readings: Handbook Chs 25, 26); Lussier & Achua (Chs 1,2,3, 4, 5 & 10; Also selected readings)

Team Leadership Skills (Lecture): Team leadership and self-managed teams. Attention is focused on types of teams, nature of self-managed teams and decision making in teams Readings/Ref: Lussier & Achua (Chs 6, 7 & 8; Also revise topics covered so far)

Crisis Management and the Learning Organisation (Lecture): Crisis leadership and management; the learning organisational and organisational learning. Readings/Ref: Lussier & Achua (Ch 12 and various readings)

Leadership of Culture & Differences (Lecture): Gender & Leadership; Women in Management, Leading & Managing Diversity, Managing multiple generations at work and aging workforce Readings/Ref: Lussier & Achua (Ch 10)

Transformational/Charismatic Leadership (Lecture): Are transformational leaders any better than charismatic leaders? What about servant leadership? Readings/Ref: Further Reading (More readings:Handbook Chs 25 , 26); Lussier & Achua (Ch 9, various readings)

Leadership, Team Cognition and Emotions (Lecture): Interpersonal and team cognition, emotions/emotional intelligence and leadership. Also, the role of leadership in the development of team mental models and team performance Readings/Ref: Further Reading (Readings:Handbook Chs 15, 16 & 17. Also see selected relevant readings on the course blackboard)
Strategic leadership and Change Management (Lecture): Also Knowledge Management and Leadership succession Readings/Ref: Lussier & Achua (Ch 11,Various readings)
Revision (Review) Readings/Ref: Lussier & Achua (The whole textbook; various readings)

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University’s assessment-related policies can be found on myAdvisor ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2910 ).

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

Medical grounds: Applications for extension on medical grounds shall be made by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and supporting documentation at the location outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course concerned.

Exceptional circumstances: Applications for extension on the grounds of exceptional circumstances shall be made to the relevant Course Coordinator/Program Director by lodging the Application for Extension of Progressive Assessment form and a personal statement outlining the grounds for the application at the location and by the due date outlined in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile for the course in which this application is made. If the exceptional circumstances are such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have complied with these conditions, a case should be made as to why these conditions could not be met.

Outcome of application: Students will be advised of the outcome of their application via their student email.

Feedback on Assessment

Feedback is essential to effective learning and students can expect to receive appropriate and timely feedback on all assessment. For a detailed explanation of the feedback you are entitled to, you should consult the policy on Student Access to Feedback on Assessment. ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.02-assessment )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the myAdvisor web site. ( http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2953 )

School of Business
Assessment Guidelines

School of Business Assessment Guidelines

Student Notices are posted progressively on the UQ Business School website http://www.business.uq.edu.au/student_notices

Reviewing Assessment

The Assessment Viewing Register procedures are:

Go to Current Student – Student Notices – Exam Viewing – online form here then log in.

Students should log in using their normal UQ name (e.g. s9990000) and password.

Policy on Consultation for Progressive Assessment

Whilst staff members are genuinely sympathetic to assessment grievances, individual reviews of progressive assessment are not feasible on a large scale. Accordingly, following the release of marks, consultation will be strictly limited as follows:

Students will be seen only in designated consultation hours, or by appointment.

No consultation will occur on the day of the release of marks.

The consultation period will be limited.

Priority at all times will be given to those students who did not achieve the pass mark for that piece of assessment.

Perusal of Final Examination Papers

Students are permitted to peruse their final examination papers but should note that this is not an opportunity to seek remarking of papers.

The sessions are scheduled during weeks 1 and 2 of the teaching week in both semester one and two; and in the case of summer examinations during weeks 1 and 2 in the following semester one.

The only opportunity for students to view their final exam paper is during the above sessions. Exam papers cannot be viewed by approaching individual staff outside of these times, except for prospective graduands.

Students completing their final courses and graduating may wish to see their final exam before graduation.

Such cases are handled on a case-by-case basis with the individual staff member.

The student will be given their exam paper and the suggested answers (or the marking guide) which they may view on site.

No exam papers may be taken out of the viewing room.

No bags may be taken into the viewing room.

At the time of viewing the exam papers, the Course Coordinator or their authorised delegate must be available in the viewing room.

Appeal Against Results

Should a student feel that the mark allocated does not reflect the standard of her/his work, then she/he is encouraged to discuss the matter with the Course Coordinator. In the event of course-based disputes not being resolved with the Course Coordinator, the student may appeal to the relevant Head of School and then the Associate Dean Academic of the BEL Faculty.

Re-marks

A student who believes the judgement of their work and the resulting mark or grade they were awarded for a piece of assessment does not reflect his/her performance as measured against the published assessment criteria, can apply for a re-mark.

The result of re-marking should not unfairly disadvantage other students.

A re-mark will be conducted once only on the piece of assessment.

In all cases, a re-mark will replace the original mark for the piece of assessment in the calculation of the final grade which could result in a higher or lower grade.

Grounds for consideration

An application for a re-mark is considered only when the student:

has sought and received feedback about their performance on the assessment from the course coordinator or lecturer concerned (or has viewed the piece of assessment and, where available, model answers/written comments or other feedback/etc); and

after receiving feedback, provides a sound written academic case to demonstrate how the mark or grade awarded for an entire task, or a discrete component of a multi-component task, does not reflect their performance against the advertised criteria and standards for that work. Students should reference the published assessment criteria for the piece of assessment and clearly show where they believe there are grounds for reconsideration of the assessment judgement.

The desire for extra marks to achieve a higher final course grade is not sufficient grounds for a re-mark.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Other
School of Business
Guidelines

Computing Facilities

Courses offered by UQ Business School utilise the internet and other computer facilities. Students should be familiar with these resources prior to undertaking a course. AskIT, available through the University of Queensland Library, offers computing help and training free to UQ students. Contact the AskIT Desk, Level 2 Duhig Bldg at St Lucia.

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
Before attempting this course, students are advised that it is important to complete the appropriate prerequisite course(s) listed on the front of this course profile. No responsibility will be accepted by the School of Business, the Faculty of Business, Economics and Law or the University of Queensland for poor student performance occurring in courses where the appropriate prerequisite(s) has/have not been completed, for any reason whatsoever.

It is the student's responsibility to attend classes and consult designated notice boards and websites for correct and up-to-date information regarding the course, including examination times. Relying on fellow students to pass on information may result in incorrect information for which the University will not be held responsible.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

Generally, students were very happy with MGTS2603. Nevertheless, based on their feedback, the major assignment now weighs 40% instead of 30%.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
Tutorial/Small Group Sessions

Tutorials/Small group sessions in this course are conducted each week commencing in Teaching Week 2. Class sizes are limited. Times and registration for these sessions are available through mySI-Net. You must sign on for your preferred session electronically. Should you experience problems, please contact the teaching staff in Teaching Week 1. Once you are included in a group, you should attend that group only each week. Changes will be permitted only in exceptional circumstances, and only after discussion with teaching staff.

It is the Course Coordinator's expectation that tutorials in this course will be conducted in a professional and conscientious manner. It is expected that every question on the tutorial sheet will be fully discussed by the tutor and that student participation will be encouraged.

Consultation

Teaching staff are there to assist you. Staff members have set consultation times during which you may see them without making an appointment. Consultation times for staff are posted on the course website and on individual staff member's doors. Consultation outside of these times is by appointment only.

If you have questions or points that require clarification, you may visit during consultation times. If staff members are unavailable, leave a message with your name and telephone number or send them an email. The course coordinator is available for consultation on Mondays. If you miss her consultation hour, please leave a clear message on 3346 8145. Otherwise, e-mail may be the best alternative way of contacting her.

Where applicable, you may also use the course's online Discussions to seek assistance from both your peers and staff. Messages will be posted on this medium regularly and you must take the time to read these each week. All content related discussions (ie announcements about the course) will take place in the Main topic that is a public forum that everyone in the course is able to access and read.

Administration

All content related questions and specific questions re the administration of this course should, in the first instance be directed to the tutor/s involved in delivery of the course. Content matters that cannot be dealt with by the tutor should then be referred to the course co-ordinator. General administrative questions (for example centrally timetabled exams, room venues, etc) may be addressed to the UQ Business School Office on Level 2 Colin Clark Building, St Lucia Campus.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

-

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

-

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

-

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

-

Other Requirements & Comments:

Grades will be allocated according to University-wide standards of criterion Based Assessment.

late_submission:
Requests for the granting of extensions must be made in writing (not via email) to the Course Coordinator at least 24 hours prior to the submission date. The request must also contain documentation satisfactorily supporting the request. If an extension is approved, the new agreed date for submission will be noted on the application together with the Course Coordinator's and the student's signatures and a copy returned to the student.

Items (for which no extension has been granted) submitted after the due date and time, incur a late submission penalty. The penalty is at the rate of 5% (0.4 of a grade) of the total available marks for that particular piece of assessment, for each calendar day or part thereof that the item is overdue.

The above late submission information does not apply to honours theses, 8 unit research theses, 4 unit research projects and 2 unit research reports. For extension and late submission information in relation to these courses see section 5.4 of the course profile.
other_assessment_information:
ASSIGNMENT SUBMISSION

All assignments are to be electronically submitted to the course Blackboard site. All instructions are in the Assessment section of the Blackboard course site. This is in addition to any other arrangement that your course coordinator instructs you in the ECP Assessment Summary.

Refer Section 6.1.

DEFERRED EXAMS

Midsemester Examinations

A student who fails to sit the mid-semester examination and/or the quizzes at the scheduled times will be awarded a mark of zero, unless the School receives a deferred request on Si-net with appropriate documentation satisfactorily explaining the student's absence. The application must be submitted either prior to the scheduled date of the midsemester exam, or no later than five days after the mid-semester examination. All deferred midsemester examinations within the Business School will take place on Saturday 23 May 2015 . A timetable for the deferred mid-semester examinations will be available early in the week commencing 18 May 2015 .

This will be the only opportunity to take the deferred midsemester examination. Any student who is granted a deferred midsemester examination and who fails to attend at the appointed time and place will be awarded a mark of zero as per the University’s General Award Rules. There is no opportunity to defer a deferred examination under any circumstances.

The process for applying for deferred midsemester exams is as follows:

Applications for deferred midsemester examinations must be made online through Si-Net. Information about the process of applying and the online form can be found via mySi-net > myRequests > Deferred Examination Requests.

You will receive an email from Si-net to your student email account indicating whether or not your application has been approved.

An email will be sent from the School the Friday after the last Saturday midsemester exam reconfirming the date and venue plus other important information.

The timetable with the times and venues will be found on the UQ Business School webpage > Student > Student Notices > under Deferred Midsemester Examination (see http://www.business.uq.edu.au/student-notices ). If there is a clash an email will be sent to the student with alternate arrangements.

Any enquiries regarding mid-semester deferred examinations should be directed to the Assessment and Examinations Officer, exams.officer@business.uq.edu.au .

Saturday midsemester examination dates:

Saturday 18 April 2015

Saturday 2 May 2015

Saturday 9 May 2015

Final Examinations

A student who is unable to sit or complete a scheduled final examination must apply for a deferred examination no later than 5 days after the date the original examination was held. Applications should be lodged at a Student Centre. Faculties may approve applications for deferred examinations under the broad headings medical, compassionate or exceptional circumstances. Deferred examinations are not automatically granted. For further information (such as evidence required and time limits) please see MyAdvisor http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/index.html?page=2995 .

SUPPLEMENTARY ASSESSMENT

Supplementary assessment is an additional opportunity to demonstrate that the learning requirements for the course have been satisfied and that the graduate attributes for the course have been attained. Supplementary assessment will be granted only where the General Award Rules and Program Rules allow.

For all UQ Business School courses supplementary assessment is weighted at 100% and covers the learning outcomes for the course. This means that the marks obtained for any progressive assessment throughout the semester are disregarded and the final result for the course is based entirely on the performance in the supplementary assessment.

The highest grade that can be received for supplementary assessment is a grade of 4.

For further information on supplementary assessment please see My Advisor http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/?page=2954.

CALCULATOR POLICY

If a calculator is permitted for use in an examination in this course, the calculator must comply with the University of Queensland Calculator Scheme. Where calculators are permitted for use in the examination, students are advised to ensure that the calculator complies with the type nominated by your course coordinator. Unless the type is unrestricted, the calculator must be either a Casio FX82 series calculator, or have an 'approved label' attached which can be obtained from the Student Centre. Please refer to myAdvisor site http://www.uq.edu.au/myadvisor/exam-calculators for information on the use of calculators in examinations.
